coredata.dc:title: Influence of external electric field regulating hydrogen adsorption on graphene quantum dots, graphene quantum dots with defects, and metal-ion-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kuamit, Thanawit
Mulya, Fadjar
Kongkaew, Sirilak
Parasuk, Vudhichai
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen storage is crucial for efficient hydrogen energy utilization, but current materials often require extreme conditions, such as low temperatures (<20.15 K) or high pressures (350–700 atm), and an ideal adsorption energy between −0.2 and −0.6 eV. This study employs density functional theory (DFT) to explore hydrogen adsorption on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), including pristine GQDs, nitrogen-substituted divacancy defect GQDs (4N-GQDs), and metal-ion-doped 4N-GQDs (M-4N-GQDs, M = Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+). Pristine and 4N-GQDs show comparable adsorption energies (−0.02 eV), while M-4N-GQDs exhibit stronger adsorption, ranging from −0.221 to −0.025 eV. Ti2+-4N-GQD achieves an optimal adsorption energy of −0.221 eV, making it highly suitable for hydrogen storage. The metal center’s charge transfer upon hydrogen adsorption influences binding strength. An external electric field (EEF) further reduces adsorption energy, promoting H2 desorption. These results highlight Ti2+-4N-GQD’s potential for regulating H2 adsorption and desorption in hydrogen storage applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen adsorption
Graphene quantum dots
Charge transfer
Electric filed
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The influence of an electric field on the desorption of H2 from Ti2+-4N-GQD

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the Ratchadapisek Somphot Fund for Postdoctoral Fellowship, Chulalongkorn University, Thailand. We are gratefully thankful to the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry (CECC), Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University, the National Electronics and Computer Technology Center (NECTEC) under the National e-Science Consortium, and NSTDA Supercomputer Center (ThaiSC) for providing computing resources.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Models of pristine and doped GQDs: (a) pristine GQD, b) 4N-GQD, and c) M-4N-GQD (M = Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+).
Top and side views of optimized structures of pristine and doped GQDs before H2 adsorption: a) pristine GQD, b) 4N-GQD, c) Ti2+-4N-GQD, d) Fe2+-4N-GQD, e) Cu2+-4N-GQD, and f) Zn2+-4N-GQD.
Top and side views of optimized structures of pristine and doped GQDs after H2 adsorption: a) pristine GQD, b) 4N-GQD, c) Ti2+-4N-GQD, d) Fe2+-4N-GQD, e) Cu2+-4N-GQD, and f) Zn2+-4N-GQD.

Definition for the distance between the mid H2 bond and GQD plane (d H2mid-GQD), distance between the mid H2 bond and metal (dH2mid-M), distance between H1 and metal (dH1-M), distance between H2 and metal (dH2-M), distance between H1 and H2 (dH1-H2), and tilting angle (θ).
Side view of Ti2+-4N-GQD under electric fields in the perpendicular direction to the molecular plane.

The optimized structures for adsorption complex between H2 and Ti2+-4N-GQD at various electric field strengths: a) = 0.0 a.u., b) = 0.002 a.u., c) = 0.004 a.u., d) = 0.006 a.u., e) = 0.008 a.u., and f) = 0.010 a.u.
The relationship between the applied electric field (EFF) and the BSSE-corrected adsorption energy (Ead-BSSE) of the desorbed H2.

Adsorption energy (Ead), deformation energy (EDef), interaction energy (ΔE), and BSSE-corrected adsorption energy (Ead-BSSE) in eV of H2 adsorbed on GQD, 4N-GQD, M-4N-GQD (M = Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) (Unit: eV).

Distance between mid H2 bond and GQD plane (dH2mid-GQD), distance between mid H2 bond and metal (dH2mid-M), distance between H1 and metal (dH1-M), the distance between H2 and metal (dH2-M), the distance between H1 and H2 (dH1-H2) (Unit: Å), and tilting angle (θ) (Unit: degree).
Mulliken charge of total H1 and H2 (qH1+H2), GQDs/4N-GQDs (qGQD/4N-GQD), and metal (qMetal) before and after H2 adsorption (Unit: a.u.).
Adsorption energy (Ead), deformation energy (EDef), interaction energy (ΔE), and BSSE-corrected adsorption energy (Ead-BSSE) of Ti2+-4N-GQDs at various electric field strengths (Unit: eV).

Distance between mid H2 bond and GQD plane (dH2mid-GQD), distance between mid H2 bond and metal (dH2mid-M), distance between H1 and metal (dH1-M), the distance between H2 and metal (dH2-M), the distance between H1 and H2 (dH1-H2) (Unit: Å).

head.ce:title: Influence of external electric field regulating hydrogen adsorption on graphene quantum dots, graphene quantum dots with defects, and metal-ion-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phyathai Rd., Patumwan, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phyathai Rd., Patumwan, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Engineering, Faculty of Advanced Technology and Multidiscipline Airlangga University, Surabaya 60115, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Advanced Technology and Multidiscipline

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Engineering, Faculty of Advanced Technology and Multidiscipline Airlangga University, Surabaya 60115, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen storage is crucial for efficient hydrogen energy utilization, but current materials often require extreme conditions, such as low temperatures (<20.15 K) or high pressures (350–700 atm), and an ideal adsorption energy between −0.2 and −0.6 eV. This study employs density functional theory (DFT) to explore hydrogen adsorption on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), including pristine GQDs, nitrogen-substituted divacancy defect GQDs (4N-GQDs), and metal-ion-doped 4N-GQDs (M-4N-GQDs, M = Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+). Pristine and 4N-GQDs show comparable adsorption energies (−0.02 eV), while M-4N-GQDs exhibit stronger adsorption, ranging from −0.221 to −0.025 eV. Ti2+-4N-GQD achieves an optimal adsorption energy of −0.221 eV, making it highly suitable for hydrogen storage. The metal center’s charge transfer upon hydrogen adsorption influences binding strength. An external electric field (EEF) further reduces adsorption energy, promoting H2 desorption. These results highlight Ti2+-4N-GQD’s potential for regulating H2 adsorption and desorption in hydrogen storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Research on hydrogen gas storage has been paid lots of attention in recent years since H2 gas is seen as a green energy source, offering an alternative to fossil fuels [1]. However, storing H2 gas requires high energy due to its incompressible nature, leading to a high carbon footprint. Despite the various applications of hydrogen, storage remains a significant challenge. Transporting hydrogen is complex due to its high energy density by weight but low energy density by volume compared to hydrocarbon gases, requiring large storage tanks [2]. Traditionally, hydrogen gases are stored in high-pressure tanks or through cryogenic cooling, processes that demand significant energy input, result in evaporative losses, and require robust storage containers. Storing hydrogen requires technical methods, such as low temperatures or high pressure, due to its lightness as a gas. Storage remains a key challenge, highlighting the need to discover new materials capable of storing hydrogen at room temperature and ambient pressure. Numerous scientific studies have been conducted to enhance hydrogen storage systems [3].

The idea of storing hydrogen in solid-state materials through chemisorption and physisorption has emerged as an alternative due to various considerations. This method requires a careful selection of materials to achieve significant storage capacity. Different materials interact with hydrogen differently, characterized by specific adsorption energies. As a result, unconventional hydrogen storage methods involving sorption onto materials have been explored [4–6]. The adsorption of hydrogen by solid materials with energies between −0.2 and −0.6 eV is suitable for room temperature adsorption and desorption [7–9]. Two-dimensional carbon materials [10–12], particularly graphene [13], have garnered interest as promising materials for hydrogen storage due to their exceptional properties like large specific surface area, high adsorption speed, and high ductility [14–16]. However, the adsorption energy between hydrogen molecules and pristine graphene does not meet the criteria for hydrogen storage at room temperature [17,18]. To enhance the interaction between graphene and H2 molecules, various techniques, such as atomic doping and defect modification, have been proposed. Embedding metal in the vacancy defects allows the modified graphene to have better adsorption for H2 compared to the pristine material. Atomic doping transforms the carbon material from saturated to unsaturated, enhancing hydrogen storage performance [19–22].

Numerous studies have delved into the impact of vacancy defects on doped graphene with adsorbed H2 molecules [23]. There is significant interest in enhancing hydrogen adsorption through N-doped carbon structures [24–26]. Rao et al. researched the effects of N-substituted graphene defects doped with light metals (Li, Na, and Ca) on H2 adsorption using DFT calculations [27]. They discovered that metal-doped N-substituted graphene defects play a crucial role in improving H2 adsorption, with Ca-doped pyrrolic graphene showing high stability in capturing H2 molecules. Petrushenko explored the interaction between hydrogen molecules and nitrogen-doped graphene by varying the number of N-doped graphene sheets (1–6N) using DFT calculations [28]. Increasing the number of N-doped graphene sheets from pristine graphene to 6N-doped graphene in a horizontal configuration led to higher adsorption energy, highlighting the potential of N-doped graphene as a novel hydrogen storage material. Additionally, Petrushenko et al. investigated H2 adsorption on pristine graphene and various nitrogen-doped graphene structures using DFT calculations [29]. Their study revealed that Npyrr-graphene and 2N-graphene exhibit H2 adsorption energies of −5.78 and −5.23 kJ/mol (−0.06 and −0.05 eV), respectively, indicating that N-doped graphene can facilitate hydrogen uptake, making it a promising material for hydrogen storage.

Researchers have explored various methods to enhance the hydrogen storage performance of vacancy-defected graphene, including substitution and doping. Metallic doping in graphene has been identified as an effective strategy in this regard [30–32]. Tabtimsai et al. conducted a study on hydrogen adsorption on graphene sheets doped with group 8B transition metals using DFT calculations [33]. They observed strong binding of all transition metal atoms to graphene, with Os showing the strongest interaction, indicating its high potential for capturing hydrogen. Bakhshi et al. proposed a technique involving the decoration of palladium on co-doped graphene sheets containing boron and nitrogen [34]. These sheets, with a hexagonal structure and double carbon vacancies, aim to enhance the interaction with hydrogen. Therefore, the incorporation of metal decoration and nitrogen doping in vacancy graphene has been shown to improve the adsorption of H2.

External electric fields have been shown to impact the properties of carbon materials significantly [35–37]. Researchers have found that applying an external electric field can enhance the binding energies of hydrogen molecules to various carbon-based materials [38–40]. Studies by Pushkar Mishra et al. and others have demonstrated the effects of external electric fields on hydrogen adsorption, with promising results in terms of increased binding energy [39]. Additionally, research by Yu et al. and Yong Han et al. has highlighted the influence of external electric fields on the adsorption of hydrogen on different graphene structures, showing that the strength and orientation of the electric field can affect the adsorption energy [41,42]. Notably, the adsorption energy of hydrogen on metal-embedded nitrogen-doped double vacancy graphene can be controlled by adjusting the electric field, making it a promising material for hydrogen molecule adsorption. Today, Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have attracted the attention of researchers due to their potential in a wide range of applications [43–45]. Furthermore, GQDs have emerged as highly promising candidates for H2 adsorption/evolution due to their unique properties and size [46,47]. Overall, the application of external electric fields shows great potential in manipulating the interactions between carbon materials and hydrogen molecules for various applications.

In this study, the interaction between GQD, 4N-divacancy defect GQD (4N-GQD), and metal-doped 4N-GQD (M-4N-GQD) with H2 molecules was explored using DFT calculations. Metal ions (Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) were incorporated into the 4N cavity of 4N-GQD to create M-4N-GQD. The impact of an external electric field on the adsorption–desorption of H2 on the most effective M-4N-GQD was also studied. The findings from this research could provide valuable insights for the development of more efficient H2 storage materials.

Density functional theory calculations utilizing hybrid meta exchange–correlation functional, M06-2X, and 6-31g(d) basis-set were carried out [42,48]. The M06-2X functional was chosen for this study because of its balanced treatment of exchange–correlation interactions and its proven capability to accurately model non-covalent interactions and dispersion effects [49,50]. The adsorption between H2 and different types of graphene quantum dots: GQD, 4N-GQD, and M-4N-GQD (where M = Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) were observed. Models of GQD, 4N-GQD, and M-4N-GQD were given in Fig. 1 . All calculations were performed at room temperature and ambient pressure using Gaussian16 software [51].

The GQD model comprised 96 carbon atoms and 24 hydrogen atoms passivating their edges. The 4N-GQD was derived from GQD by removing two adjacent carbon atoms, which were then replaced by four nitrogen atoms to create divacancy defects. The 4N-GQD provided favorable sites for anchoring single metal ions (Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) forming M-4N-GQD. We chose C96H24, also known as circumcircumcoronene, as the model of GQD because it has a hexagonal shape and is symmetric, which is more suitable for modeling adsorption [43]. Besides, C96H24 was used in our previous study on the effect of external electric field on the curvature of GQDs [37]. Geometry optimizations and energy calculations were performed with a self-consistent field tolerance set at 10−6 Hartree to ensure convergence. Additionally, Mulliken population analysis was performed to quantify the amount of charge transferred during H2 adsorption, both before and after the process.

Adsorption energy (Ead), which quantifies the strength of the adsorption between H2 molecule and GQD, can be determined from:
(1)
E
a d
=
E
c o m p l e x
-
E
G Q D / b f
-
E
H 2 / b f
where Ecomplex, EGQD/bf, and EH2/bf are energies of the complex between GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD and H2, GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD before adsorption, and H2 before adsorption, respectively. Thus, the positive value of calculated Ead represents an unfavorable H2 adsorption. The adsorption energy determined according to Eq. (1) is known to be overestimated due to Basis Set Superposition Error (BSSE). The BSSE correction based on the counterpoise correction method by Boys and Bernardi [52] was calculated and added to adsorption energies, represented by Ead-BSSE.

Interaction energy (ΔE) signifies the binding interaction between H2 and GQD and can be evaluated using
(2)
Δ E =
E
c o m p l e x
-
E
G Q D / a f
-
E
H 2 / a f
where Ecomplex, EGQD/af, and EH2/af are energies of the complex between GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD and H2, GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD after adsorption, and H2 after adsorption, respectively. The relation between Ead and ΔE is given by
(3)
E
a d
= Δ E +
E
D e f
EDef is the deformation energy, which represents the energy used for deforming structures of GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD and H2 after adsorption. This equation is arrived at by taking Eqs. (1) and (2) incorporating the definition of the deformation energy.

All geometry optimizations were carried out using M06-2X/6-31g(d). Previous research has indicated that the M06-2X functional, which partially incorporates dispersion correction, is best for non-covalent interactions [50,53,54]. The first-row transition metals, specifically Ti, Fe, Cu, and Zn, with a focus on their +2 oxidation states and the differences in their d orbital electron configurations, were investigated. Ti has the fewest d electrons, Fe has an intermediate number, while Cu and Zn are electron-rich. Structures of GQD, 4N-GQD, and M-4N-GQD before and after H2 adsorption, as well as the position of H2, were monitored and displayed in Figs. 2 and 3
, respectively. Before H2 adsorption, optimized structures of GQD, 4N-GQD, and M-4N-GQD are mostly planar, except for Zn2+- and Ti2+-4N-GQD where the ion is located above the plane in the middle of the cavity and the structure of the graphene quantum dot ligand is slightly curved. Thus, the metal-ion substitution causes the curving of the 4N-GQD structure in the case of Zn2+ and Ti2+. After H2 adsorption, structures of GQD/4N-GQD/M (Fe2+, Cu2+)-4N-GQD remain planar. While for Ti2+-4N-GQD, the quantum dot becomes convex-like with Ti2+ still above the structure. However, for Zn2+-4N-GQD, the structure is concave-like, and Zn2+ lays below the structure. In all structures, the adsorbed H2 molecule was found to align itself parallel to the surface, see Fig. 3. The H2 adsorption could induce curvature in the structure as in the cases of Ti2+ doping.

Adsorption energy (Ead), deformation energy (EDef), interaction energy (ΔE), and BSSE-corrected adsorption energy (Ead-BSSE) of H2 adsorbed on pristine GQD, 4N-GQD, and 4N-GQD doping with (Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+), were presented in Table 1 . Apart from Ti2+-4N-GQD, adsorption energy is less than −0.1 eV for other materials, but it is still in the binding region (negative adsorption energy). The observation is consistent with the experimental work of Wang et al. [25]. For Ti2+-4N-GQD, Ead-BSSE is −0.221 eV, which lies in the suitable region for adsorption and desorption. The results indicate that Ti2+-4N-GQD doping, being the transition metal with the lowest electron count in the first row, performs better than the others. Thus, Ti2+-4N-GQD could be appropriately used for H2 adsorption. The results of previous studies [55] suggest that Ti-doped graphene displays superior performance for H2 adsorption compared to Zn, Zr, and Al, which is directly relevant to the findings of this work. Analysis of the BSSE-corrected adsorption energy found that the Ead-BSSE of Ti2+-4N-GQD contains a very high deformation energy of 0.124 eV, see Table 1. This accords with the increase in the curvature of Ti2+-4N-GQD after H2 adsorption, as shown in Figs. 2c and 3c. Although the adsorption causes large deformation of the structure, its Ead-BSSE remains very large due to strong interaction energy (−0.392 eV). While for other structures, the deformation energy is very small or negligent. Thus, the interaction energy is the only contribution of the adsorption. Interestingly, Zn2+-4N-GQD also has a curve structure, but its EDef is close to zero. Because EDef is evaluated from the energy difference of structures before and after H2 adsorption. In this case, the adsorption does not initiate more deformation (Figs. 2f and 3f).

To understand why Ti2+-4N-GQD binds more strongly to H2 than other compounds, geometrical parameters such as distances between the mid of H2 bond and GQD plane (dmidH2-GQD)/metal (dmidH2-M), distances between H1/H2 and metal (dH1-M/ dH2-M), and H2 bond distance (dH1-H2) before and after adsorption were monitored. These geometrical parameters are given in Table 2 . From Table 2, we found dH1-M/ dH2-M of all compounds not equal. This suggests that H2 does not align parallel to the GQD plane for the adsorption with M-4N-GQD but is slightly tilted. From Fig. 2, we did not observe this tilting of H2 for GQD and 4N-GQD. The tilting angle (θ) for all structures was evaluated according to Fig. 4 , and their values were listed in Table 2.

The tilting angle for H2 is between 1.650 and 4.070, where Zn2+-4N-GQD provides the smallest, and Ti2+-4N-GQD gives the largest values. We did not see any relation between Ead and θ nor Ead and dmidH2-GQDs. However, we could see the linear relationship of the distance between mid H2 bond and M and BSSE-corrected adsorption energies (with the regression coefficient R2 = 0.985 for the linear least square fit between the two values) when the values for Cu2+-M-4N-GQD are exempted, as displayed in Fig. S1. For M-4N-GQD, the binding of H2 to metal is more reasonably addressed with dmidH2-M than dmidH2-GQD since the metal ion does not lie on the GQD plane. Except for Ti2+, the metal ion lies above the GQD plane, and the shortening of dH2mid-M is noticed. From this analogy, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ are 0.029, 0.047, and 0.328 Å under the plane, respectively, while Ti2+ is 0.458 Å above. The H2 bond elongation was also noted only for the Ti2+ system, which could be reasoned with a strong electron transfer from the H2 bond.

From the previous section, there is a hint that electron transfer might play a role in the H2 adsorption of GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD. Therefore, the charge transfer between H2 and adsorbent was monitored. Table 3 displayed Mulliken charges of H2 and GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD before and after adsorption, as well as their charge differences (Δq). The positive/negative value of Δq signifies electron loss/gain. The behavior of M-4N-GQD for electron transfer with H2 is distinct from GQD/4N-GQD. For GQD/4N-GQD, H2 accepts electrons from the quantum dot, while for M-4N-GQD, H2 transfers its electrons to the material. For M-4N-GQD, we found that the transfer electrons are distributed to both quantum dots and metal with an M:4N-GQD ratio of 80:20. This phenomenon could be explained as follows. GQD/4N-GQD are electron-rich and could transfer their electrons to H2. The metal ion is electron-deficient and can receive electrons. The adsorption energy has a relation with the number of transferred electrons. Thus, 4N-GQD has a stronger Ead than GQD, and Ti2+-4N-GQD, with the largest number of transferred electrons from H2, has the largest Ead. For M-4N-GQD, there is a linear relationship between Δq of metal (Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) before and after H2 adsorption and Ead-BSSE with R2 of 0.998 as shown in Fig. S2. (There is also the relation between Δq and ΔE with R2 = 0.999, as shown in Fig. S3.) Thus, the binding of H2 is related to the transferring of charges. For Ti2+-4N-GQD, H2 loses the largest number of electrons, which weakens its bond, resulting in bond elongation. The dependence of charge on Ead-BSSE implies that for this system, the adsorption of H2 is controlled by the electrostatic interaction. The number of d electrons seems to affect the charge transfer. Since Ti is d electron deficient, it tends to be able to accept more electrons from hydrogen. Hence, its binding with H2 is enhanced.

It should be noted that Mulliken’s population analysis might not be able to describe the charge distribution accurately. We additionally, therefore, carried out other population analysis methods such as Natural Bond Orbital (NBO), Atomic Polar Tensor (APT), and Electrostatic Potential (ESP) to assess this. The results are given in Tables S1–S3 of the supplementary. Only APT shows the same trend of Δq as the Mulliken analysis. However, all methods report Ti2+-4N-GQD to yield the highest Δq, indicating the strongest H2 adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of an electric field on the desorption of H2 from Ti2+-4N-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: In the previous section, we recommended Ti2+-4N-GQD for use as an H2 storage material since it has adsorption energy within the suitable range. From the energy decomposition of Ead, we found a very large deformation energy owing to its curve structure, though having a much larger interaction energy. Furthermore, our previous work on the effect of external electric field (EEF) on the curving of GQDs suggested that GQDs can be curved upon exposure to EEF, and their curvature increases with the strength of the field [37]. Since the curving of GQDs requires high energy, EDef would increase with the curvature of the quantum dot, which then reduces Ead-BSSE. Thus, by introducing more curving to Ti2+-4N-GQD, H2 can be desorbed from the complex. Therefore, we are interested in seeing the effect of EEF on the H2 adsorption on Ti2+-4N-GQD. Here, EEF with the field strength of 0.000, 0.002, 0.004, 0.006, 0.008, and 0.010 a.u. were applied to the adsorption complex between H2 and Ti2+-4N-GQD in the direction perpendicular to the molecular plane as shown in Fig. 5 . Here, both positive and negative fields were applied.

The effect of EFF on the curvature of the 4N-GQD ligand is displayed in Fig. 6 . Based on Fig. 6, the curvature of the 4N-GQD ligand exhibits a slight increase as the field strength rises. This effect of EFF on the planarity of the structure is similar to that of GQDs [37]. The curvature increment of the adsorption complex between Ti2+-4N-GQD and H2 upon EEF reflects its EDef, which is shown in Table 4 . Table 4 lists adsorption energy (Ead), deformation energy (EDef), interaction energy (ΔE), and BSSE-corrected adsorption energy (Ead-BSSE) of Ti2+-4N-GQD with H2 at external electric field strengths ranging from 0.000 to 0.010 a.u with 0.002 a.u. interval. The results indicate that the adsorption energy tends to be less negative (weaker) under the positive external electric field (EEF), favoring H2 desorption. Conversely, under a negative external electric field, the adsorption energy tends to be more negative (stronger), favoring H2 adsorption. The increase of Ead with negative EEF corresponds to the rise of the binding (ΔE), while the field causes little change for EDef.

Under positive field conditions, we observed the increment of EDef from 0.124 to 0.502 electron volts (eV). The increase of EDef causes Ead to be raised (being more positive) as well, from −0.268 to +0.215 eV (−0.221 to +0.274 for Ead-BSSE). Indeed, H2 molecules can be desorbed from Ti2+-4N-GQD when applying EEF. Interestingly, the increment of Ead-BSSE is larger than the increase of EDef at the same field strength. For example, at 0.010 a.u. while EDef rises by 0.378 eV (from zero field strength), Ead-BSSE enlarged by 0.495 eV. Though the interaction between H2 and Ti2+-4N-GQD is weakened with the field strength, from −0.392 to −0.287 eV, H2 still binds strongly to Ti2+-4N-GQD. Thus, the weakening of H2 binding is reasoned for a stronger increase of Ead-BSSE versus EDef with field strength. Fig. 7 illustrates that Ead-BSSE becomes positive when EEF strength is greater than 0.006 a.u. In other words, the H2 molecule could be desorbed from Ti2+-4N-GQD when applying field strength greater than 0.006 a.u. The dependence of interaction energy on EEF was reported in other previous works [39,41,56]. However, they found the interaction energy to be weakened with the applied positive field. Distances between the mid of the H2 bond and the GQD plane (dmidH2-GQD)/metal (dmidH2-M), distances between H1/H2 and metal (dH1-M/ dH2-M), and H2 bond distances (dH1-H2) before and after adsorption were listed in Table 5 . We found the distance between the metal and hydrogen and the GQD plane and hydrogen to increase when increasing the external electric field. Therefore, the adsorption of H2 can be controlled by adjusting the external electric field. However, here, we only apply the positive field direction. Since the negative field direction might have an alternated effect on the adsorption energy, as reported in previous works [38–40]. This work is linked to the research conducted by Yong Han et al., which proposes that the adsorption/desorption of H2 on Li-functionalized N-doped defective graphene can be regulated through an external electric field [42].

In this study, we have explored the non-covalent interaction between molecular hydrogen and three types of materials: pristine graphene quantum dot (GQD), four nitrogen-substituted divacancy defect graphene quantum dot (4N-GQD), and 4N-GQD doping with various metal ions, i.e., Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+, (M-4N-GQD) employing density functional theory calculations. Among these materials, Ti2+-4N-GQD demonstrated the highest affinity for adsorbing H2 molecules, while pristine GQD displayed the lowest capacity for H2 adsorption. We do not observe a clear relationship between the distance of H2 to the GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD plane (dmidH2-GQD/4N-GQD/M-4N-GQD) and adsorption energy with (Ead-BSSE). This is probably because, for M-4N-GQD, the metal ion does not lie on the plane of 4N-GQD. A better relationship is noticed between the distance of H2 to metal (dmidH2-M) and Ead-BSSE (with regression coefficient R2 of 0.985 when excluding results for Cu2+ for the linear least square fit between the two values). However, our findings revealed the linear relationship between the adsorption energy of the H2 molecule and the charge difference (Δq) of the metal ion center of M-4N-GQD. Thus, the linear least square fit between Ead-BSSE and Δq shows a regression coefficient R2 of 0.998. This Δq represents the charge transfer to/from the H2 molecule. Thus, greater adsorption energy aligns with a larger charge transfer from the H2 to the M-4N-GQD plane. The findings indicate that Ti2+-4N-GQD outperforms other systems, with an adsorption energy of −0.221 eV (BSSE-corrected), which falls within the ideal range for hydrogen storage (−0.2 to −0.6 eV). It also exhibits the shortest distance between the H2 molecule and the GQD plane, the highest electron density, and superior charge transfer capabilities. These results highlight the exceptional storage capacity of Ti2+-4N-GQD, making them well-suited for hydrogen storage applications. Interestingly, the high adsorption energy consists of large and positive deformation energy, which coincides with its curve structure after H2 adsorption. We knew GQD could curve more by interaction with an external electric field [37]. Thus, applying an external electric field to the system could allow the desorption of H2. Therefore, we conducted observations of H2 desorption from Ti2+-4N-GQD under various electric field strengths, ranging from 0.002 to 0.010 atomic units (a.u.). The increase in electric field strengths corresponds to an increase in the adsorption energies of Ti2+-4N-GQD (becoming more positive). The correlation between the applied electric field (EFF) and the BSSE-corrected adsorption energy (Ead-BSSE) suggests that H2 can desorb when the applied electric field exceeds 0.006 a.u. This promising finding regarding Ti2+-4N-GQD strongly suggests its capability to effectively regulate both the adsorption and desorption of hydrogen (H2). Although Ti2+-4N-GQD is further curved under EEF, the structure can tolerate an even stronger field without losing its integrity [37]. Therefore, it can be reused for adsorption and desorption of H2. These unique characteristics position the material as an excellent candidate for applications as hydrogen storage. Their ability to precisely manage the uptake and release of hydrogen makes them particularly valuable for enhancing the efficiency and performance of hydrogen storage systems.

Thanawit Kuamit: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fadjar Mulya: Validation, Methodology. Sirilak Kongkaew: Writing – review & editing. Vudhichai Parasuk: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the Ratchadapisek Somphot Fund for Postdoctoral Fellowship, Chulalongkorn University, Thailand. We are gratefully thankful to the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry (CECC), Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University, the National Electronics and Computer Technology Center (NECTEC) under the National e-Science Consortium, and NSTDA Supercomputer Center (ThaiSC) for providing computing resources.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.comptc.2024.115050.

Throssell, J.A. Montgomery Jr., J.E. Peralta, F. Ogliaro, M.J. Bearpark, J.J. Heyd, E.N. Brothers, K.N. Kudin, V.N. Staroverov, T.A. Keith, R. Kobayashi, J. Normand, K. Raghavachari, A.P. Rendell, J.C. Burant, S.S. Iyengar, J. Tomasi, M. Cossi, J.M. Millam, M. Klene, C. Adamo, R. Cammi, J.W. Ochterski, R.L. Martin, K. Morokuma, O. Farkas, J.B. Foresman, D.J. Fox, Gaussian 16 Rev. C.01, in, Wallingford, CT, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The calculation of small molecular interactions by the differences of separate total energies. Some procedures with reduced errors
Mol. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.F. Boys, F. Bernardi, The calculation of small molecular interactions by the differences of separate total energies. Some procedures with reduced errors, Mol. Phys. 19 (1970) 553-566, DOI: 10.1080/00268977000101561.
